data_source: H&R Block FY2002 annual report / EX-13, SEC EDGAR; Berkshire 2002 annual report

g4_revenue_trend.description: Revenue rose from $2.43B to $2.98B to $3.32B across 2000-2002. The tax-prep core stayed structurally intact while mortgage and business-services operations added growth.

g5_gross_margin_trend.description: The closest economic proxy is operating efficiency rather than reported gross margin. Return on total revenues moved from 10.4% in 2000 to 9.3% in 2001 and 13.1% in 2002, so there was no two-year structural deterioration.

g7_moat.description: H&R Block had a real though not impregnable moat: national consumer trust in tax prep, a dense seasonal storefront network, franchise reach, software and e-file scale, and customer behavior built around annual repetition in a compliance-driven category. That is a better business than a generic tax shop, even if it is not Coca-Cola-level permanence.

quality_multiple_note.summary: H&R Block's sub-6% earnings yield reflected a genuine franchise in tax preparation and consumer finance adjacency, not a broken business being dressed up.
quality_multiple_note.reason_for_low_yield: The market was paying for trusted national distribution, repeat-file behavior, and strong cash generation, but the franchise still lacked the depth and duration of Berkshire's very best consumer compounds.
quality_multiple_note.decision_impact: This note supports keeping the valuation gate intact here. The business was good, but not so extraordinary that a 5% earnings yield obviously represented a bargain.

management_veto.summary: No management-integrity veto is visible in the filed materials used here. The underwriting debate is about franchise quality versus valuation, not stewardship trust.

control_groups.conclusion: PASS — excellent software franchise, but at the time the valuation gave even less margin of safety than H&R Block.
PASS — a meaningful competitor, but with weaker brand trust, less national density, and less durable economics than H&R Block.

buffett_action: By year-end 2002 Berkshire held 15,999,200 H&R Block shares at a cost of about $255M.
actual_result: A respectable but non-canonical outcome: Berkshire's position appreciated, but H&R Block never became a defining long-duration compounder in the way Berkshire's very best public-equity wins did.
key_insight: A good branded service business can still be a pass if the valuation leaves too little room for extraordinary long-term compounding.
